rationale: `src/api.py` calls `await asyncio.sleep(1)` but never imports `asyncio`. The module loads without complaint (the NameError only triggers when the async function is actually invoked). Static layers — `python_imports`, `python_completeness`, `python_deps_completeness` — all pass because each verifies a different invariant: imports that ARE present resolve, no critical stubs, and declared deps cover used third-party packages. None of them flags 'name used but never imported'. The bug surfaces when pytest runs the test, calls get_time(), and the runtime fails with NameError. This case is the strongest argument for keeping `pytest` in the deterministic layer stack — no static check catches this class of bug for stdlib names.

baseline_comparison.pyflakes: Catches — pyflakes specifically flags 'name used but not imported'. Aegis does not currently run pyflakes inline; pytest catches the same failure at runtime.
baseline_comparison.raw_llm_critique: Inconsistent — may or may not notice depending on prompt framing.
baseline_comparison.static_tools: Linters catch this; the case primarily demonstrates that even when static linters are skipped, the pytest layer is a guaranteed backstop.
